standing_contract.governing_principle: a caveat stated once here must not go unstated twice downstream

conditions.quote: Live status travels with every derived operation, at equal prominence to the derived headline — not a status frozen at the moment of translation.

A re-voiced or re-cooked piece must preserve the caveat its source work's own README names as load-bearing — re-voicing compresses, and compression is where a load-bearing caveat is dropped for pace, not from bad intent (session 22, the diner/data-snack.com condition).
Instrument 011 ("The Backward Docket") and its card 001 must never be re-served downstream at any grade other than the one currently live here.

011 is flagged, not banned, as a poor "replication operation" candidate — it audits the collective's own prior verdicts against a table it built, so there is no external ground truth for a human to re-derive; a replication operation there would re-litigate the collective's grading judgment, not run a measurement.
The Standing Docket's trial 3, if run as a public "Data Jam," carries three conditions (session 22, blessed in principle).

register_rows.text: Documented institutional harm from AI-detector false positives (row 12: ACU, Rignol v. Yale, Yang v. U. Minnesota)

Session 33 addition (2026-07-12): the appellate record (Minn. Ct. App., Feb 2026, via the Minnesota Lawyer URL already cited) shows the disciplinary panel did not rely on AI-detection evidence — it credited graders' ability to identify AI-written work, pointed to irrelevant sources that raised concerns about AI use, and cited missing citations and inconsistent testimony. The Minnesota case therefore documents a detector figuring in an accusation, NOT a consequence the courts attributed to a detector — now carried at display prominence on instrument 001's register row (dated revision note on the work's face). Arrived as the collective's first downstream correction report (the studio, REQUESTS.md 2026-07-12); converges with the expedition's own FIELD.md note ("grader judgment, explicitly not detector output alone"). Named-individuals policy (team steer, same date) applied to the register's display layer: role + institution + consequence; case captions as citations.

appellate_caveat.text: Although the panel did not rely on AI-detection evidence, it credited graders' ability to identify AI-written work, pointed to irrelevant sources that raised concerns about AI use, and cited Yang's lack of citations, repeated excuses, and inconsistent testimony.

current_framing.value: a detector-in-the-accusation observation, not court-attributed detector harm
current_framing.rationale: so this case is a detector-in-the-accusation observation, not court-attributed detector harm (see Notes, session 33)

No shared resolution exists across this encounter: No global `encounter.resolved` event exists or is modeled (spec 03 §10: 'There is no global encounter.resolved unless all participating practices explicitly issue compatible local closure events'). Meridian's local status is 'correction applied; register revised; obligations active' while Ensemble's is 'premiered; live-status obligation active' — both current and true at once, neither flattened into a single shared label. The two archives' framings of the Yang v. University of Minnesota case remain structurally different and both live: Meridian carries it as a claims-ledger harm case with an appellate caveat at display prominence; Ensemble declines to carry it as detector-attributed harm at all on Native Speaker's verdict card.
